texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: On A Moving Average With Internal Degrees of Freedom
texts.text: On A Moving Average With Internal Degrees of Freedom

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linda Boudjemila University of Science and Technology Houari Boumedien Bab Ezzouar Algiers, Algeria, 16111
texts.text: Linda Boudjemila University of Science and Technology Houari Boumedien Bab Ezzouar Algiers, Algeria, 16111

texts.label: text
texts.orig: bobyl@theory.ioffe.ru
texts.text: bobyl@theory.ioffe.ru

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -Anew type of moving average is developed. Whereas a regular moving average (e.g. of price) has a built-in internal time scale (time-window, exponential weight, etc.), the moving average developed in this paper has the weight as the product of a polynomial by window factor. The polynomial is the square of a wavefunction obtained from an eigenproblem corresponding to other observable (e.g. execution flow I = dV/dt , the number of shares traded per unit time). This allows to obtain an immediate 'switch' without lagging typical for regular moving average.
texts.text: Abstract -Anew type of moving average is developed. Whereas a regular moving average (e.g. of price) has a built-in internal time scale (time-window, exponential weight, etc.), the moving average developed in this paper has the weight as the product of a polynomial by window factor. The polynomial is the square of a wavefunction obtained from an eigenproblem corresponding to other observable (e.g. execution flow I = dV/dt , the number of shares traded per unit time). This allows to obtain an immediate 'switch' without lagging typical for regular moving average.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Index Terms -Radon Nikodym, fat tails, non-Gaussian noise
texts.text: Index Terms -Radon Nikodym, fat tails, non-Gaussian noise

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the modern world, when conducting experiments and measurements, data processing is of critical importance [1], [2], [3], [4]. To solve various problems, the data obtained as a result of measurements or calculations are grouped and further processed [5], [6], [7], [8]. One of these options is timeseries. Timeserie data is widely available and used. A typical problem in data analysis to study timeserie properties and, possibly, trying to build a predictive model. Built model are typically of autoregression, Kalman filter[9], [10], [11], [12], [13], hidden Markov model[14], [15], [16], [17], [18], [19], [20], and others[21]. In this work we consider timeserie data of M past observations, only past observations l = 1 . . . M are available. The value of M increases with every new observations coming. A typical data of this type is timeserie data of financial transactions. The most commonly available data is (Time, Execution Price, Shares Traded) sequence of transactions that we will be using for demonstration. The problem of moving average[22], [23], [24], [25], [26], [27], [28], [29] is considered. Whereas typically considered moving average has a builtin internal time scale (time-window, exponential weight, etc.), the moving average developed in this paper has the weight dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt with ψ ( x ) being a polynomial obtained from eigenvalue problem corresponding to a spike in other observable, such as execution flow I = dV/dt . This allows to obtain an immediate 'switch' without lagging. In previous work [30], [31] the 'switch' was obtained by applying an advanced technique of secondary sampling, where a calculated value was used as it were a new observable for sampling. In this work we have implemented 'switching' regime without Vadim Davydov Peter the Great St.Petersburg Polytechnic University Polytechnicheskaya 29 St. Petersburg Russia, 195251 davydov vadim66@mail.ru Vladislav Malyshkin Ioffe Institute Polytechnicheskaya 26 St. Petersburg, Russia, 194021
texts.text: In the modern world, when conducting experiments and measurements, data processing is of critical importance [1], [2], [3], [4]. To solve various problems, the data obtained as a result of measurements or calculations are grouped and further processed [5], [6], [7], [8]. One of these options is timeseries. Timeserie data is widely available and used. A typical problem in data analysis to study timeserie properties and, possibly, trying to build a predictive model. Built model are typically of autoregression, Kalman filter[9], [10], [11], [12], [13], hidden Markov model[14], [15], [16], [17], [18], [19], [20], and others[21]. In this work we consider timeserie data of M past observations, only past observations l = 1 . . . M are available. The value of M increases with every new observations coming. A typical data of this type is timeserie data of financial transactions. The most commonly available data is (Time, Execution Price, Shares Traded) sequence of transactions that we will be using for demonstration. The problem of moving average[22], [23], [24], [25], [26], [27], [28], [29] is considered. Whereas typically considered moving average has a builtin internal time scale (time-window, exponential weight, etc.), the moving average developed in this paper has the weight dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt with ψ ( x ) being a polynomial obtained from eigenvalue problem corresponding to a spike in other observable, such as execution flow I = dV/dt . This allows to obtain an immediate 'switch' without lagging. In previous work [30], [31] the 'switch' was obtained by applying an advanced technique of secondary sampling, where a calculated value was used as it were a new observable for sampling. In this work we have implemented 'switching' regime without Vadim Davydov Peter the Great St.Petersburg Polytechnic University Polytechnicheskaya 29 St. Petersburg Russia, 195251 davydov vadim66@mail.ru Vladislav Malyshkin Ioffe Institute Polytechnicheskaya 26 St. Petersburg, Russia, 194021

texts.orig: one can calculate (with double precision arithmetic) the moments to a very high order m ≲ 50 (limited by the divergence of c jk m multiplication coefficinets (11)) in Laguerre basis and m ≲ 150 (limited by poorly conditioned matrices) in shifted Legendre basis; Chebyshev polynomials T m (2 x -1) also provide very stable calculations in shifted Legendre basis (Chebyshev polynomials have perfectly stable multiplication: all c jk m = 0 except c jk j -k = c jk j + k = 0 . 5 , j ≥ k ). All obtained results are invariant with respect to basis choice, Q m ( x ) = x m and the ones from (6) give identical results, but numerical stability can be drastically different[32], [33].

All obtained results are invariant with respect to basis choice, Q m ( x ) = x m and the ones from (6) give identical results, but numerical stability can be drastically different[32], [33].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: III. ON AVERAGING OF PAST OBSERVATIONS
texts.text: III. ON AVERAGING OF PAST OBSERVATIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider familiar demonstration with a moving average. Let P τ be a regular exponential moving average. The average 〈·〉 is calculated with the weight (2):
texts.text: Consider familiar demonstration with a moving average. Let P τ be a regular exponential moving average. The average 〈·〉 is calculated with the weight (2):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P τ ( t now ) = 〈 pI 〉 〈 I 〉 = 〈 Q 0 pI 〉 〈 Q 0 I 〉 = ∫ t now -∞ dV ω ( t ) p ( t ) ∫ t now -∞ dV ω ( t ) (7)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The averaging dµ = ω ( t ) dt takes place between the past and t now using exponentially decaying weight ω ( t ) = exp( -( t now -t ) /τ ) . With τ increase, the contributing to integral interval becomes larger and moving average 'shifts to the right' ( τ -proportional time delay, lagging indicator). The (7) has no single parameter that can 'adjust' the time scale, see Fig. 1 for a demonstration. From this demonstration it is clear that all moving average dependencies are smooth, with the time scale of τ .
texts.text: The averaging dµ = ω ( t ) dt takes place between the past and t now using exponentially decaying weight ω ( t ) = exp( -( t now -t ) /τ ) . With τ increase, the contributing to integral interval becomes larger and moving average 'shifts to the right' ( τ -proportional time delay, lagging indicator). The (7) has no single parameter that can 'adjust' the time scale, see Fig. 1 for a demonstration. From this demonstration it is clear that all moving average dependencies are smooth, with the time scale of τ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider a different approach. Introduce a wavefunction ψ ( x ) as a linear combination of basis function Q k ( x ) :
texts.text: Consider a different approach. Introduce a wavefunction ψ ( x ) as a linear combination of basis function Q k ( x ) :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ψ ( x ) = n -1 ∑ k =0 α k Q k ( x ) (8)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then an observable market-related value f , corresponding to the probability density ψ 2 ( x ) , is calculated by averaging timeserie sample with the weight dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt ; the expression corresponds to an estimation of Radon-Nikodym derivative[34].
texts.text: Then an observable market-related value f , corresponding to the probability density ψ 2 ( x ) , is calculated by averaging timeserie sample with the weight dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt ; the expression corresponds to an estimation of Radon-Nikodym derivative[34].

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: f ψ = n -1 ∑ j,k =0 α j 〈 Q j | f | Q k 〉 α k n -1 ∑ j,k =0 α j 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 α k (10)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For averages we use bra-ket notation by Paul Dirac: 〈 ψ | and | ψ 〉 . The (9) is plain ratio of two moving averages, but the weight is not regular decaying exponent ω ( t ) from (2), but exponent multiplied by wavefunction squared as dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt , the ψ 2 ( x ) defines how to average a timeserie sample. Any ψ ( x ) function is defined by n coefficients α k , the value of an observable variable f in ψ ( x ) state is a ratio of two quadratic forms on α k (10). Regular moving average (7) corresponds to ψ ( x ) = const . Whereas typical approaches (Fourier, least squares, linear regression, wavelets, etc.) deals with vector of moments 〈 Q m f 〉 , m = 0 . . . n -1 , the quadratic forms ratio (10) operates with matrices 〈 Q j | f | Q k 〉 and 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 . The matrices can be obtained from 〈 Q m f 〉 , m = 0 . . . 2 n -2 , moments using multiplication operator:
texts.text: For averages we use bra-ket notation by Paul Dirac: 〈 ψ | and | ψ 〉 . The (9) is plain ratio of two moving averages, but the weight is not regular decaying exponent ω ( t ) from (2), but exponent multiplied by wavefunction squared as dµ = ψ 2 ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) dt , the ψ 2 ( x ) defines how to average a timeserie sample. Any ψ ( x ) function is defined by n coefficients α k , the value of an observable variable f in ψ ( x ) state is a ratio of two quadratic forms on α k (10). Regular moving average (7) corresponds to ψ ( x ) = const . Whereas typical approaches (Fourier, least squares, linear regression, wavelets, etc.) deals with vector of moments 〈 Q m f 〉 , m = 0 . . . n -1 , the quadratic forms ratio (10) operates with matrices 〈 Q j | f | Q k 〉 and 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 . The matrices can be obtained from 〈 Q m f 〉 , m = 0 . . . 2 n -2 , moments using multiplication operator:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Q j Q k = j + k ∑ m =0 c jk m Q m (11)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main idea of [33] is to consider a wavefunction (8) then to construct (10) quadratic forms ratio. A generalized eigenvalue problem is then considered with the two matrices from (10).
texts.text: The main idea of [33] is to consider a wavefunction (8) then to construct (10) quadratic forms ratio. A generalized eigenvalue problem is then considered with the two matrices from (10).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We established, that execution flow I = dV/dt (the number of shares traded per unit time), not trading volume V (the number of shares traded), is the driving force of the market: asset price is much more sensitive to execution flow I (dynamic impact), rather than to traded volume V (regular impact). This corresponds to the matrices 〈 Q j | I | Q k 〉 and 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 . These two matrices are volume- and time- averaged products of two basis functions. Generalized eigenvalue problem for operator I = dV/dt is then:
texts.text: We established, that execution flow I = dV/dt (the number of shares traded per unit time), not trading volume V (the number of shares traded), is the driving force of the market: asset price is much more sensitive to execution flow I (dynamic impact), rather than to traded volume V (regular impact). This corresponds to the matrices 〈 Q j | I | Q k 〉 and 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 . These two matrices are volume- and time- averaged products of two basis functions. Generalized eigenvalue problem for operator I = dV/dt is then:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: n -1 ∑ k =0 〈 Q j | I | Q k 〉 α [ i ] k = λ [ i ] n -1 ∑ k =0 〈 Q j | Q k 〉 α [ i ] k (13)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our analysis shows that among the states ∣ ∣ ψ [ i ] 〉 of the problem (12) the state corresponding to the maximal eigenvalue among all λ [ i ] , i = 0 . . . n -1 , is the most important for market dynamics. Consider various observable characteristics in this state ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 .
texts.text: Our analysis shows that among the states ∣ ∣ ψ [ i ] 〉 of the problem (12) the state corresponding to the maximal eigenvalue among all λ [ i ] , i = 0 . . . n -1 , is the most important for market dynamics. Consider various observable characteristics in this state ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Fig. 2 a demonstration of several observables is presented: the price in ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 state (15), maximal eigenvalue λ [ IH ] of (12) problem, and minimal eigenvalue λ [ IL ] (for completeness).
texts.text: In Fig. 2 a demonstration of several observables is presented: the price in ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 state (15), maximal eigenvalue λ [ IH ] of (12) problem, and minimal eigenvalue λ [ IL ] (for completeness).

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P [ IH ] = 〈 ψ [ IH ] ∣ ∣ pI ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 〈 ψ [ IH ] ∣ ∣ I ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 (15)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From these observable one can clearly see that singularities in I cause singularities in price, and that a change in ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉
texts.text: From these observable one can clearly see that singularities in I cause singularities in price, and that a change in ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉

texts.orig: localization causes an immediate 'switch' in an observable. This switch is caused by the presence of n -1 internal degrees of freedom α k ( n coefficients (14), one less due to normalizing 1 = 〈 ψ | ψ 〉 ). Such a 'switch' is not possible in regular moving average (7) since it has no any internal degree of freedom, hence, all regular moving average dependencies are smooth.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: IV. ON LOCALIZATION CHANGES IN ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉
texts.text: IV. ON LOCALIZATION CHANGES IN ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considered above the state ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 maximizes the number of shares traded per unit time on past observations sample; it determines the time scale. Let us consider in this state not the price and execution flow as we studied before, but simply time distance to 'now' T [ IH ] :
texts.text: Considered above the state ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 maximizes the number of shares traded per unit time on past observations sample; it determines the time scale. Let us consider in this state not the price and execution flow as we studied before, but simply time distance to 'now' T [ IH ] :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: T [ IH ] = 〈 ψ [ IH ] ∣ ∣ ( t now -t ) I ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 〈 ψ [ IH ] ∣ ∣ I ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 (16)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: T τ = 〈 ( t now -t ) I 〉 〈 I 〉 (17)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: to compare it with regular moving average T τ (were it an integral over dt the T τ would be a constant; the invergation with dV make it tracking the spikes in I ). As all the values of time (future and past) are known, the (16) carry information about ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 localization. When the value is small - a large dV/dt spike event happened very recently. When it is large - a large spike happened a substantial time ago, the value is an information when a large spike in dV/dt took place. In Fig. 3 the value of T [ IH ] (scaled by the factor 10 -3 and shifted up to fit the chart) is presented along with regular moving average T τ for τ = 256 s. One can clearly see that there is no smooth transition between the states, the 'switch' happens instantly, there is no τ -proportional time delay, what is typical for regular moving averages T τ and the one in Fig. 1. A linear dependence of T [ IH ] on time is also observed, this is an indication of stability of ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 state identification. The value of T [ IH ] is the time scale; typically it is easier to work with the density matrix ρ J ( ψ 2 ) obtained from ψ ( x ) = ψ [ IH ] ( x ) rather than with the time scale itself.
texts.text: to compare it with regular moving average T τ (were it an integral over dt the T τ would be a constant; the invergation with dV make it tracking the spikes in I ). As all the values of time (future and past) are known, the (16) carry information about ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 localization. When the value is small - a large dV/dt spike event happened very recently. When it is large - a large spike happened a substantial time ago, the value is an information when a large spike in dV/dt took place. In Fig. 3 the value of T [ IH ] (scaled by the factor 10 -3 and shifted up to fit the chart) is presented along with regular moving average T τ for τ = 256 s. One can clearly see that there is no smooth transition between the states, the 'switch' happens instantly, there is no τ -proportional time delay, what is typical for regular moving averages T τ and the one in Fig. 1. A linear dependence of T [ IH ] on time is also observed, this is an indication of stability of ∣ ∣ ψ [ IH ] 〉 state identification. The value of T [ IH ] is the time scale; typically it is easier to work with the density matrix ρ J ( ψ 2 ) obtained from ψ ( x ) = ψ [ IH ] ( x ) rather than with the time scale itself.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A moving average with the weight dµ = dV Φ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) is considered.
texts.text: A moving average with the weight dµ = dV Φ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) is considered.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P aver = ∫ t now -∞ dV Φ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) p ( t ) ∫ t now -∞ dV Φ( x ( t )) ω ( t ) (18)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ω ( t ) is decaying exponent (2); the polynomial Φ( x ) is obtained solely from observed execution flow I = dV/dt and has the form Φ( x ) = ψ [ IH ] 2 ( x ) , it corresponds to the maximal eigenvalue of (12) eigenproblem. Contrary to regular moving average (7) the developed approach has n -1 internal degrees of freedom what adjusts averaging weight according to spikes in other observable (such as execution flow I = dV/dt ). These internal degrees of freedom allow to obtain an immediate 'switch', what is not possible in regular moving average that always has τ -proportional time delay, lagging indicator. The comparison with regular moving average is most clear in Fig. 3: smooth regular moving average (blue line) vs. 'switching' moving average with internal degrees of freedom (green line).
texts.text: The ω ( t ) is decaying exponent (2); the polynomial Φ( x ) is obtained solely from observed execution flow I = dV/dt and has the form Φ( x ) = ψ [ IH ] 2 ( x ) , it corresponds to the maximal eigenvalue of (12) eigenproblem. Contrary to regular moving average (7) the developed approach has n -1 internal degrees of freedom what adjusts averaging weight according to spikes in other observable (such as execution flow I = dV/dt ). These internal degrees of freedom allow to obtain an immediate 'switch', what is not possible in regular moving average that always has τ -proportional time delay, lagging indicator. The comparison with regular moving average is most clear in Fig. 3: smooth regular moving average (blue line) vs. 'switching' moving average with internal degrees of freedom (green line).
